narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 76% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows strong potential in some areas, like your undergraduate GPA of 3.10—which is just a bit below the typical 3.21 for students who pass—but it highlights challenges in others, including your Year 1 school decile ranking in the bottom 10%, Year 3 decile in the bottom 20%, a notably low LSAT score of 20 compared to the average 37, lower-middle family income, Asian racial background, male gender, and plans to attend part-time. When the model weighed these details against historical data, your Year 3 decile ranking emerged as the single biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, more than any other aspect of your background. Right behind it was your LSAT score, which had nearly as strong an influence in signaling higher risk. Your Year 1 decile came next in importance, followed by family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, part-time status, and gender—all of which, in your specific case, contributed toward expecting a struggle on the bar rather than a pass. Typical students who go on to pass the bar on their first try rank around the middle of their class in both early and later years (deciles near 5 or 6), score much higher on the LSAT, often come from higher income brackets on average, and are more commonly full-time white students—making your lower rankings and scores stand out as bigger departures from that successful profile. While no single feature dooms an application, this combination shifted the odds firmly against you. The good news is you can take concrete steps to shift those odds for a future application or bar prep. Prioritize retaking the LSAT to aim for a score closer to 35 or higher, as it's one of the top influencers and something you can directly improve with focused study. Similarly, building evidence of stronger academic performance—perhaps through postgraduate courses or detailed explanations of your early low deciles—could help counter those early signals. Finally, exploring full-time study options might align better with patterns of bar success, giving you a clearer path forward. Keep pushing; many students turn predictions like this around with targeted effort.
